subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Brasserie du Pêcheur: The Sufficiently Serious Breach

questions.question: Brasserie du Pêcheur SA v Germany and R v Secretary of State for Transport, ex parte Factortame Ltd (Joined Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93) were decided in which year, and what was their combined significance?

options.A: 1993; they first established the principle of state liability
options.B: 1996; they confirmed that state liability applies to all branches of government and defined the 'sufficiently serious breach' test
options.C: 1999; they extended state liability to judicial acts for the first time
options.D: 2001; they limited state liability to cases where the Commission had previously identified the breach

questions.explanation: Joined Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93, decided in March 1996, are the key cases extending and refining Francovich. Brasserie du Pêcheur (German beer purity rules incompatible with free movement of goods) and Factortame III (UK Merchant Shipping Act incompatible with free movement of establishment) together confirmed: (1) state liability extends beyond Directive non-implementation to any breach of EU law; (2) the 'sufficiently serious breach' test is the key condition; (3) national courts must award damages where the test is met.

questions.question: The 'sufficiently serious breach' test in Brasserie du Pêcheur was modelled on which pre-existing legal standard?

options.A: The standard for EU institutional liability under Article 340 TFEU — a 'sufficiently flagrant violation of a superior rule of law protecting individuals'
options.B: The standard for criminal liability in member states — proof beyond a reasonable doubt
options.C: The standard for interim injunctions — a 'serious question to be tried'
options.D: The standard for proportionality review — 'manifestly inappropriate'

questions.explanation: The Court in Brasserie explicitly modelled the member state liability test on the EU institutional liability standard under Article 215 EEC (now Article 340 TFEU — Schöppenstedt formula), which required a 'sufficiently flagrant violation of a superior rule of law protecting individuals'. The Court held it would be inconsistent for the EU to hold its own institutions to a high threshold while permitting member states to be liable for any breach, however minor. The single test ensures coherent standards across the EU legal order.

questions.question: The central factor in determining whether a breach is 'sufficiently serious' according to the Brasserie test is:

options.A: Whether the member state acted in bad faith
options.B: Whether the member state manifestly and gravely disregarded the limits on its discretion
options.C: Whether the Commission had issued a reasoned opinion criticising the measure
options.D: Whether more than three individuals suffered loss

questions.explanation: The core Brasserie test is whether the member state 'manifestly and gravely disregarded the limits on its discretion'. This formulation focuses on the degree to which the member state exceeded or ignored the boundaries of its discretion under EU law. A breach is more likely to be sufficiently serious when the member state had no real discretion, or when a prior CJEU ruling made the incompatibility clear. Minor or reasonable errors in areas of genuine ambiguity may not meet the threshold.

questions.question: Which of the following factors did the Court in Brasserie du Pêcheur identify as relevant to assessing whether a breach is 'sufficiently serious'?

options.A: The size of the member state's economy and its GDP per capita
options.B: The clarity and precision of the EU rule breached; the degree of discretion left to the member state; whether the infringement was intentional or involuntary; whether an error of law was excusable; and whether any CJEU ruling or Commission position had indicated incompatibility
options.C: Whether the Parliament had adopted a resolution condemning the breach
options.D: Whether the individual suffered personal injury or only economic loss

questions.explanation: The Brasserie factors for assessing 'sufficiently serious breach' include: (1) clarity and precision of the rule breached; (2) measure of discretion left to national or EU authorities; (3) whether the infringement and damage caused were intentional or involuntary; (4) whether any error of law was excusable or inexcusable; (5) the fact that a position taken by an EU institution may have contributed to the adoption of the national measure; (6) any adoption or retention of national measures contrary to EU law. No single factor is determinative.

questions.question: Where a member state has little or no discretion under EU law, what is the consequence for the 'sufficiently serious breach' test?

options.A: The member state cannot be held liable — absence of discretion means absence of culpability
options.B: The mere infringement of EU law may by itself constitute a sufficiently serious breach
options.C: The claimant must additionally prove intent to discriminate
options.D: The member state has an automatic defence if it relied on legal advice

questions.explanation: The Court in Brasserie confirmed that where a member state has little or no legislative discretion under EU law, a mere infringement of that law may suffice to establish a sufficiently serious breach. For example, if an EU Regulation is crystal clear and allows no derogation, a member state's non-compliance with it may automatically satisfy the threshold without additional analysis. This makes sense: where no discretion exists, any departure is by definition a manifest disregard of the legal boundary.

questions.question: In the Factortame III element of the joined cases, the UK's Merchant Shipping Act 1988 had imposed nationality requirements on fishing vessel registration. The CJEU's approach to the 'sufficiently serious breach' was:

options.A: The breach was not sufficiently serious because the UK had acted in good faith based on economic concerns
options.B: The breach was sufficiently serious — the discriminatory nationality requirements clearly violated established free movement principles, leaving little doubt as to incompatibility, and the incompatibility had been confirmed in earlier Factortame rulings
options.C: The breach was not sufficiently serious because the Merchant Shipping Act predated the relevant EU free movement rules
options.D: The question of 'sufficient seriousness' was remitted to the national court without CJEU guidance

questions.explanation: The UK's Merchant Shipping Act nationality requirements were clearly incompatible with EU free movement rights — the incompatibility had already been confirmed by the CJEU (Factortame I and II). Given prior rulings confirming incompatibility and the clear EU law on non-discrimination, the CJEU held that the UK had manifestly disregarded the limits on its discretion. The national court was left to apply the test, but the CJEU's analysis strongly indicated the breach was sufficiently serious.

questions.question: Can a member state escape state liability by arguing that its national legislature, rather than the executive, made the decision that breached EU law?

options.A: Yes — state liability applies only to executive acts, not legislative measures
options.B: No — the Court held in Brasserie that state liability applies regardless of which organ of the state caused the breach; the state is a unity for EU law purposes
options.C: Yes — only if the legislature acted under a constitutional obligation inconsistent with EU law
options.D: Partially — legislative breaches attract lower compensation under national procedural autonomy principles

questions.explanation: Brasserie confirmed that state liability extends to all organs of the state — legislature, executive, and judiciary. The state is treated as a unity for EU law purposes. A member state cannot argue that it is not liable because the breach was caused by its national parliament rather than the government. This was significant in both Brasserie (German legislative measure) and Factortame III (UK Act of Parliament). No distinction is drawn between the branches of government for liability purposes.

questions.question: The third condition for state liability — 'direct causal link' — means:

options.A: The breach must have been the sole cause of the loss
options.B: There must be a sufficiently direct causal connection between the breach of EU law and the damage suffered by the individual — intervening causes may break the causal chain
options.C: The Commission must have directly caused the breach through incorrect guidance to the member state
options.D: The individual must have been in direct contact with the member state at the time the breach occurred

questions.explanation: The causal link condition requires that the breach of EU law be the direct cause of the loss. This mirrors standard tort law causation requirements. National courts apply this condition, considering whether the loss would not have occurred but for the breach, and whether any intervening events broke the causal chain. Where a member state's failure to implement a Directive directly denied the claimant an economic benefit they would have received, causation is straightforward. Complex causal chains may be harder to establish.

questions.question: Can a member state rely on its own constitutional rules or domestic legal difficulties as a justification for non-compliance with EU law, so as to avoid the 'sufficiently serious breach' finding?

options.A: Yes — if national constitutional law made compliance impossible, the breach cannot be sufficiently serious
options.B: No — a member state cannot rely on provisions, practices, or situations of its domestic legal system to justify failure to comply with EU law
options.C: Yes — provided the constitutional conflict was genuine and unforeseen
options.D: Only if the Constitutional Court has issued a formal ruling finding the conflict irresolvable

questions.explanation: The CJEU has consistently held that a member state cannot invoke its domestic constitutional rules, national legal traditions, or internal difficulties as justification for non-compliance with EU law. This applies equally in the state liability context: if a member state breaches EU law because of internal constitutional constraints, the breach may still be 'sufficiently serious' if it manifestly disregards EU law. The state is responsible for ensuring its domestic legal order complies with EU obligations.

questions.question: What type of damage can be recovered under state liability in EU law?

options.A: Only pure economic loss — personal injury and property damage are excluded
options.B: Any type of loss or damage causally connected to the breach, whether economic, personal, or property loss — subject to national rules on quantum (themselves subject to equivalence and effectiveness)
options.C: Only loss of the specific right the Directive would have conferred — no consequential losses
options.D: Punitive damages, to deter future breaches

questions.explanation: State liability covers all forms of damage causally connected to the breach — economic loss, personal injury, property damage, and other losses. The quantum of damages is determined by national courts applying national rules on assessment of damages, subject to the EU principles of equivalence and effectiveness. National courts must ensure reparation is adequate — it cannot be merely symbolic or so low as to be an insufficient deterrent to non-compliance. Exemplary (punitive) damages may also be available if they would be awarded in an equivalent domestic claim.

questions.question: The 'sufficiently serious breach' threshold in Brasserie was criticised by some commentators as being too difficult to satisfy. What is the principal policy argument in favour of a high threshold?

options.A: To protect national parliaments from judicial interference in legislative decisions
options.B: To avoid making member states over-cautious and to preserve space for good-faith legislative choices in areas where EU law is genuinely uncertain
options.C: To ensure that only the Commission, not private individuals, can enforce EU law against member states
options.D: To limit the financial exposure of poorer member states with fewer resources to comply

questions.explanation: The 'sufficiently serious' threshold preserves space for good-faith legislative action. If any minor misjudgment about EU law by a legislature gave rise to unlimited liability, member states would become excessively cautious, potentially paralysing government. The high threshold is designed to catch clear and inexcusable breaches while protecting reasonable legislative choices in genuinely uncertain areas. The tension between effective enforcement and leaving space for legitimate legislative action is the key policy balance the test strikes.

questions.question: In Brasserie du Pêcheur, Germany argued that liability should be conditional on proof of fault (faute de service) by the responsible national authority. The CJEU's response was:

options.A: The Court accepted that fault was a necessary condition, consistent with general principles of liability
options.B: The Court rejected fault as a condition: whether a breach is 'sufficiently serious' is an objective assessment based on the factors it identified, not a subjective fault inquiry — national courts cannot add a fault requirement
options.C: The Court held that fault was relevant only to the quantum of damages, not to liability
options.D: The Court deferred to national law on the question, allowing Germany's fault requirement to stand

questions.explanation: Germany argued that state liability should require proof of faute de service — an administrative fault concept in French and German law. The CJEU rejected this, holding that the 'sufficiently serious breach' test it had established was an autonomous EU law condition — national courts could not add a fault requirement. The objective assessment of seriousness replaces (not supplements) any national fault inquiry. This is essential to ensuring uniform standards of liability across all member states.

questions.question: Where a prior CJEU ruling has found that a member state's measure breaches EU law, and the member state retains the measure, what is the effect on the 'sufficiently serious breach' assessment?

options.A: No effect — the original assessment must be repeated from scratch
options.B: It strongly indicates (or may virtually compel the finding) that continued non-compliance is a sufficiently serious breach — the prior ruling removes any possible claim that the incompatibility was unclear
options.C: The liability claim is automatically successful without further analysis
options.D: The member state has six months to comply before liability can arise

questions.explanation: A prior CJEU ruling establishing incompatibility is a powerful factor — indeed, in many cases it will make a finding of 'sufficiently serious breach' virtually inevitable for continued non-compliance. Once the CJEU has ruled that a measure is contrary to EU law, it is impossible for the member state to claim it was genuinely uncertain about the position. Retention of the incompatible measure after such a ruling is strong evidence of manifest and grave disregard of the limits on discretion.
